---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## When One Treated Unit Breaks DiD

### Learning objectives

- Build a synthetic control from a weighted donor pool
- Use convexity to avoid extrapolation and fake fits
- Infer with placebo permutation when sample size is one
- Reach for synthetic DiD when a trend match suffices

::: notes
Welcome to Week Four, Chapter four-point-four: synthetic control and synthetic difference-in-differences. Chapter four-point-one gave you difference-in-differences, and it gave you a warning. In this opening part we frame the lesson. Difference-in-differences compares the change in a treated group to the change in a control group, and it identifies the causal effect only if the two groups would have moved in parallel had the policy never happened. That parallel-trends assumption is the entire engine. But what do you do when the world hands you just one treated unit? That is the question this chapter answers.
:::

## DiD Needs Many Units With Matching Trends

- Dozens of treated units, dozens of controls
- Pre-trends lying convincingly on top of each other
- The event-study plot from 4.1 makes that case visible
- Parallel trends becomes a bet you can defend
- Point at the pre-period; show the lines tracking

::: notes
When you have dozens of treated units and dozens of controls, and their pre-treatment trends lie convincingly on top of one another, parallel trends is a bet you can defend. You point at the pre-period, show the lines tracking, and argue that absent the policy they would have kept tracking. The event-study plot from section four-point-one is exactly that argument made visible. That is the comfortable world: many units, a clean comparison group, and a pre-period you can display to a skeptic. The trouble starts when that comfortable world disappears, and you are left with a single treated unit and no obvious group to compare it against.
:::

## Sometimes You Get Exactly One

- One state, one firm, one country adopts the policy
- Pick one similar control? A critic finds another
- Average all controls equally? No reason that matches
- Pre-trends rarely line up for one specific unit
- Parallel trends becomes a **wish**, not a claim

::: notes
But sometimes the world hands you one treated unit. A single state passes a first-in-the-nation law. A single firm gets hit by a scandal. A single country adopts the euro, or builds a wall, or unifies after a partition. Now the comfortable machinery of difference-in-differences starts to creak. Which control do you use? If you pick one similar state, your estimate lives or dies on that one arbitrary choice, and a critic will always find a different similar state that gives the opposite answer. If you average all the other states into a single control group, you are implicitly weighting them equally, and there is no reason equal weights should reproduce your treated unit's trajectory. The pre-trends almost never line up, because no naturally occurring group is a good twin for one specific unit. Parallel trends becomes a wish rather than a defensible claim. This chapter is about what to do when one treated unit is all you have.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Difference-in-differences leans on **parallel trends**, which you can defend when many treated units share a clean control group. With **one** treated unit there is no natural twin: picking a control is arbitrary, averaging equally is unjustified, and parallel trends collapses into a wish. Next: the central idea — build a twin.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: difference-in-differences works by comparing a treated group's change to a control group's change, and it leans entirely on parallel trends. When you have many treated units and a clean control group whose pre-trends track, you can defend that assumption by displaying it. But with a single treated unit, the assumption collapses. Picking one similar control is arbitrary, averaging all controls equally has no justification, and the pre-trends almost never line up for one specific unit. Parallel trends becomes a wish. In part two we meet the idea that rescues us: instead of picking or averaging a control, we build one.
:::
